Name: dynamic_kernel_adaptation
Title: Dynamic Kernel Adaptation for Transformer Inference Based on Profiled Input Characteristics
Short Hypothesis: Transformer inference latency can be significantly reduced by dynamically adapting CUDA kernel implementations based on real-time profiling of input sequence lengths and memory access patterns.
Related Work: Existing works like EvoEngineer and AutoKernel focus on automating kernel optimization or reducing launch overhead, but they do not address the issue of kernel performance degradation due to varying input sequence lengths. Our proposal introduces a runtime feedback loop between hardware profiling and kernel adaptation, which is a novel approach not covered in the literature.
Abstract: Transformer models have become ubiquitous in machine learning, but their inference latency remains a critical bottleneck, especially when dealing with variable-length inputs. This paper proposes a dynamic kernel adaptation framework that leverages real-time hardware profiling to select optimal CUDA kernel implementations based on input sequence lengths and memory access patterns. By continuously profiling key metrics such as warp stall reasons, achieved bandwidth, and arithmetic intensity, our framework dynamically switches between pre-optimized kernels for different input regimes. We demonstrate significant latency reductions on an NVIDIA A100 GPU for transformer inference tasks, with up to 2.5x speedup compared to static kernel implementations. Our approach bridges the gap between model compression and deployment systems engineering by providing a hardware-faithful solution that adapts to real-world input variability.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Profiling baseline transformer inference kernels across a range of input sequence lengths (10 to 1000 tokens) to identify performance bottlenecks.
Experiment 2: Developing and optimizing specialized kernels for short, medium, and long sequence regimes based on profiled metrics.
Experiment 3: Implementing a runtime adaptation mechanism that switches between kernels based on profiled input characteristics.

Experiment 4: Evaluating end-to-end inference latency, throughput, and energy efficiency on a standard transformer model (e.g., BERT) using dynamic kernel adaptation compared to static kernel implementations.
Metrics: P50/P99 latency, tokens-per-second, energy-per-token, warp stall reasons, achieved bandwidth, arithmetic intensity.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential overhead from runtime profiling and kernel switching may offset performance gains for very short sequences.
The effectiveness of dynamic adaptation may vary across different GPU architectures.
The approach requires extensive profiling and kernel optimization effort for each new model architecture.
Limited applicability to models with fixed-length inputs or non-variable workloads.
